text.title: Generative UI Tools and Generation Workflow
text.summary: Tool landscape, generation loop, quality rules, and human/agent responsibility split for generative UI design.

list.item: v0 by Vercel — REST API for component generation; output is React/TSX; agent-callable
Claude Artifacts — native in-context generation; interactive HTML/React; agent-callable
Galileo AI — browser-only; no public API; image-based output (not editable component trees)
Uizard — browser-based; no headless API; image-based output
Relume — Figma plugin + browser; no API; optimized for marketing/SaaS site structure

rule.statement: Generate 3–5 variants per brief, not 1. Single-variant generation anchors the team too early and forecloses design space exploration.
rule.rationale: The purpose of generative UI is to expand the option space cheaply. A single variant is no better than having a designer start from scratch — it still anchors the conversation to one direction.

rule.statement: Treat generated UI as a zero-cost rough draft. Critique aggressively; accept nothing without review.
rule.rationale: Generated output that is accepted without critique carries forward AI defaults (generic aesthetics, missing accessibility, deprecated patterns) into production.

example.code: 1. Agent generates 3-5 variants (parallel calls, different style constraints) 2. Human selects variants, marks off-brand elements, flags a11y issues 3. Agent refines selected variant based on critique notes 4. Human runs axe-cli / accessibility audit on refined output 5. Human verifies against brand style guide 6. Approved → isolated branch; marked with AI-generated comment

rule.statement: Run axe-cli or equivalent on all generated HTML before presenting to stakeholders.
rule.rationale: Generated UIs that look accessible routinely have broken focus order, missing live regions, and contrast failures. Automated check is mandatory before any stakeholder presentation.

rule.statement: Pin design tokens explicitly in every generation prompt. Without them, AI defaults to generic Bootstrap-like aesthetics.
rule.rationale: AI generation models have no brand knowledge; explicit token references (color names, type scale, spacing) are the only way to anchor output to the design system.

rule.statement: Keep generated components isolated from the production codebase until manually reviewed and refactored.
rule.rationale: Generated code frequently uses deprecated APIs, missing responsive breakpoints, and incorrect composition patterns; direct merge without review introduces technical debt.

rule.statement: Mark all AI-generated components with a comment: "AI-generated — review before production".
rule.rationale: Unmarked AI-generated code becomes invisible in the codebase; marking enables future audits and prevents accidental promotion to production.

antipattern.description: Using Galileo or Uizard output as Figma-import-ready components.
antipattern.reason: Both produce image-based outputs, not editable component trees; importing into Figma requires manual rebuild from scratch.

antipattern.description: Using generative UI for design system contributions.
antipattern.reason: Generated components bypass token governance, variant structure, and composition rules; they require significant rework to meet design system standards.
